text: The surgeon performs a robotic hiatal hernia repair and Toupee fundoplication, dissecting the gastrohepatic ligament to the level of the right crus. The right crus is dissected and skeletonized, exposing the esophagus.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the short gastric arteries up to the level of the left cruce. The attachments of the stomach to the left-sided cruce are also severed, freeing the diaphragmatic hiatus.

The surgeon performs mediastinal dissection of the esophagus using electrocautery and blunt dissection techniques. A Penrose drain is visibly passed around the esophagus to aid in the dissection process.

The surgeon cleans the decussation of the crura, removing all attachments. The posterior esophagus is mobilized using a harmonic scalpel and blunt dissection, freeing it from posterior attachments. The aorta is visible at the bottom of the screen.
The surgeon carefully navigates around the fat tissue on the esophagus, taking care to avoid injury to the anterior vagus nerve, which is visible within this tissue.

The robotic system facilitates a high mediastinal dissection and mobilization of the esophagus, reaching the level of the inferior pulmonary veins. The left-sided and right-sided inferior pulmonary veins are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon re-approximates the diaphragmatic hiatus using O-ethabon sutures, completing the hiatal hernia repair portion of the operation.

The surgeon performs a Toupet fundoplication by wrapping the stomach posteriorly around the esophagus, using the shoeshine maneuver. The stomach is then secured to the diaphragmatic hiatus at both the left and right crura, completing the setup for the fundoplication.
Two sutures are placed from the stomach to the esophagus on either side, with the anterior vagus nerve visible coursing between the fundoplication.
